case_name: Teresa Grimes Kidd v. James Q. Dickerson

summary.brief: Teresa Grimes Kidd v. James Q. Dickerson. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: On October 21, Ms. Grimes fainted and was seen at the Maury Regional Medical Center emergency room and admitted to the hospital for further examination and treatment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A consultation with a radiation oncologist was conducted on October 24, who diagnosed her with a stage IV 'malignant neoplasm of fundus of stomach'.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ms. Grimes’ pathology 'returned with invasive gastric adenocarcinoma'; 'prior imaging had revealed a mass in liver which is now consistent with metastatic disease.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: An MRI on October 24 revealed 'multiple strokes consistent with embolic etiology.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ms. Grimes was subsequently diagnosed with 'healthcare associated pneumonia' and later transferred to a nursing home; she remained non-communicative and non-responsive until she expired on November 21, 2014.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On September 30, 2014, 82-year-old Doris Grimes went to the Family Health Group ('Family Health Group' or 'FHG') in Columbia, Tennessee, complaining of pain and swelling in her lower left arm; she was seen by her primary care physician, Dr. Charles Ball, who diagnosed her with a blood clot.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On October 7, she returned to the practice, complaining of a sudden onset of pain behind her right knee. She was seen by another doctor in the practice, Dr. Thomas Farmer, who ordered further diagnostic evaluations; he concluded that Ms. Grimes had a blood clot and prescribed a blood-thinning medication known as 'Pradaxa.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ms. Grimes returned to Family Health Group on October 13 for a follow-up appointment relative to swelling in her leg. Her medical chart recites that she was 'placed on one week of [P]radaxa and made appointment with [Dr. Ball] after seeing Dr. Farmer.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The chart records that Ms. Grimes stated that her leg 'does get tender and sore if she stands to[o] long,' and that she has 'a little bit of swelling at times.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The entry for October 13 states that Dr. Ball observed that the swelling in her leg was 'now completely resolved,' that he changed her prescription to Xarelto, 20 milligrams daily, and set a one month follow-up appointment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On October 20, Ms. Grimes returned and was seen by a nurse practitioner, Shavonne Frierson; at that visit, an exam was conducted, which resulted in findings that Ms. Grimes was 'ill appearing' and her cardiovascular rhythm was 'irregularly irregular.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A chest x-ray was conducted, and an entry in her chart states that she was to be referred to a cardiologist 'this week.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court granted summary judgment to the pharmacy defendants on all claims, holding that the proof submitted by Plaintiff was insufficient to establish the element of causation.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court granted summary judgment to Dr. Thomas Farmer in toto.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We affirm the grant of summary judgment to the pharmacy defendants; we affirm the grant of summary judgment to Dr. Thomas Farmer in toto.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We affirm in part the grant of partial summary judgment to the doctors and their group and remand for further proceedings on whether the nurse practitioner’s actions caused Ms. Grimes’ injury and suffering during the period of October 20 until she was stabilized in the hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Arthur Axelbank, who opined that Ms. Grimes’ death was 'directly referable to the progressive and cumulative effects of the loss of blood caused by continued ingestion of Pradaxa, which led to severe intestinal bleeding.'
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: The trial court appropriately granted summary judgment in favor of Defendants Thomas William Farmer, M.D., Charles Albert Ball, M.D., or Family Health Group, Inc. as to Plaintiff’s wrongful death claim.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court appropriately granted summary judgment in favor of Defendants Thomas William Farmer, M.D., Charles Albert Ball, M.D., or Family Health Group, Inc., as to Plaintiff’s 'hastening of death' claim.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court appropriately granted summary judgment in favor of Defendants Thomas William Farmer, M.D., Charles Albert Ball, M.D., or Family Health Group, Inc. as to Plaintiff’s informed consent claim.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.chief_complaint: Pain and swelling in lower left arm
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: On September 30, 2014, Doris Grimes presented with pain and swelling in her lower left arm. She was diagnosed with a blood clot.
initial_state.initial_working_problem: Blood clot in lower left arm

reveal.result_summary: Multiple strokes consistent with embolic etiology
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt further investigation into embolic sources

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with pain and swelling in lower left arm

timeline_phases.name: Follow-up and Monitoring
timeline_phases.description: Patient returns for follow-up and monitoring of blood clot treatment

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with history of blood clot and on blood-thinning medication
decision_points.prompt: What should be done to monitor the effects of Pradaxa?

options.description: Regular monitoring of blood levels and signs of bleeding
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to monitor anticoagulation therapy
Neglects potential side effects of anticoagulation

expected_action_court.description: Regular monitoring should have been conducted
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated need for monitoring

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to monitor increased risk of undetected complications
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Regular monitoring could have detected bleeding earlier
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient experienced pain and suffering due to undiagnosed GI bleeding

key_findings: Failure to monitor closely led to pain and suffering

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Closer monitoring could have reduced suffering
